generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower. The sea is restless, the sky a canvas of black and silver, and the waves crash with a fury that seems to echo the storm in my heart. I have not seen you in five years, and I fear that tonight may be the last time I speak to you at all.

You were a child when you left, and though I did not see you off, I watched from the cliffs as your carriage disappeared into the mists. I told myself it was for the best, that you would find your own path, your own way in this world. But the years have passed, and I have grown old in this lonely place, with only the sea and the wind for company.

I have not written to you, not in all these years, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But I must try. You were my only joy, my light in the darkest nights, and I have missed you more than words can say. I have missed your laughter, your presence, the way you would sit by the window and tell me stories of the world beyond these rocks.

I have not spoken of it, but I have been thinking of you often. I find myself staring at the horizon, wondering if you are safe, if you are happy. I have heard of the great cities, the bustling streets, the grandeur of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows of this old lighthouse as if it, too, knows the storm that is to come. The sea, that endless, restless thing, roars in fury, and I find myself wondering if it is the sea or my soul that is most out of control.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of letters unanswered, of visits unreturned, of a father’s love that has grown cold in the absence of your presence. I have tried to forget, to move on, to pretend that the hurt of your departure was but a passing storm. But the storm has returned, and it is not the sea that I fear most now—it is the thought of you, so far away, never knowing that I still wait for you, still hold you in my heart.

I have always been the keeper of this light, you know. Not just the light that guides the ships through the darkest nights, but the light that guides me through the darkest hours of my life. And yet, tonight, I feel that light flickering, uncertain, as if it may be snuffed out by the very winds that have always been my companions.

I have not slept in days, watching the waves crash against the rocks, listening to the cries of the gulls, feeling the weight of the world upon my

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken that I take up this quill. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet the silence between us feels as vast as the sea that surrounds this lighthouse. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the urgency of a man who knows that time is running out.

Tonight, the storm approaches with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I have seen tempests before, and I have weathered them, but this one feels different. It is as though the heavens themselves are in mourning, and I fear that I may not see the dawn.

I have spent my life in service to this lighthouse, tending to its light, guiding the ships that pass in the night, and keeping the darkness at bay. But now, I wonder if I have been keeping more than just the darkness away. I have kept my sorrow, my guilt, and the silence that has grown between us. I have kept my love from you, and I fear that I may have kept it too long.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care. I know the years have not been kind to

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, and the wind howling like a beast from the abyss. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is black as a man’s heart. I’ve lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but I fear it may not be enough to keep the dark at bay—either from the sea, or from my soul.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of your name fading from my lips like the last embers of a dying fire. I have not spoken of you to a soul, not even to the old man who once shared this keep with me. He’s gone now, and the lighthouse stands alone, like I stand alone.

You were my light, Eliza. Not the lamp that guides the lost, but the light in my heart, the warmth in the cold, the reason I kept going when the world felt too heavy to bear. And yet, you left me. Left me with nothing but the salt air and the sound of waves, and the memory of your laughter—so distant now, like a ghost on the shore.

I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the day you came to see me, with your mother’s shawl wrapped around you, and your eyes full of questions and fear. You were so young then, and I was old, and we spoke of the sea, and

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you angry, or perhaps even indifferent. I have not written to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here in this lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, I find myself compelled to speak. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. If I do not, I would have you know that I have not forgotten you, and that I have not forgotten the pain of our parting.

Five years ago, you left this place with a suitcase and a heart full of resentment. You called me a fool, a man too stubborn to see the world beyond the sea. You said I was blind to the life you wished for, and that I had no soul. I do not deny that I was wrong, in many ways. I was blind, yes, but not to the world—only to you. I was blind to the love I held for you, and to the truth of your heart.

I used to think that the light of the lighthouse would guide the ships safely through the night, but I never considered that it might also guide the way back to you. I have spent these years watching the sea, hoping that one day, the tide would turn, and you would return. But you did not. You chose your path, and I chose mine.

And yet, here I am, still

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.

I know you would call me a fool, perhaps even a monster, for the way I have turned my back on you. I know you believe I have forgotten you, that I have let the sea and the stone take precedence over the bond we once shared. But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the way your laughter echoed through this lighthouse when you were a child, the way your small hands clutched mine as we watched the stars blink above the waves. I have not forgotten, Eleanor.

You were seven when you left. I was not cruel, nor did I mean to push you away. I thought I was protecting you. The world is a cruel place, and I feared it would take you from me. I believed that if I kept you close, if I kept you safe, I could shield you from the storms of life. But I was wrong. And in my misguided love, I drove you away.

I have been alone since. The lighthouse has been my only companion, the sea my only friend. I have watched the sun rise and set

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea rages, and the sky is a bruised mass of black and green, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I hope you will know that I did not write this letter in haste, nor with regret—only with the weight of years, and the truth that I have long kept buried.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have not dulled the ache of your absence. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to forget the way your eyes turned away when I spoke your name, the way you said you were “too old for a man like me.” I have tried to believe that the sea would one day take me, and that you would not have to bear the burden of my shame.

But the sea is not so cruel. It has spared me, and now it threatens to take me by force. I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms. But this one… this one feels different. It feels like the end of something. Perhaps the end of me, perhaps the end of the world as I know it.

I do not know if you will ever read this. If you have not come back, then perhaps you do not wish to. But I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the stone that holds the lighthouse together. It has been five years since I last saw you, and five years is a long time to let silence grow between a father and a daughter. I have not spoken to you, not written to you, not even dared to think of you. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I find myself reaching for the quill, as if the storm might carry my words to you.

You were not the daughter I raised. You were the daughter I *wanted* to raise—bright, spirited, with a mind that burned like a candle in the night. But you chose your own path, and I chose mine. I became a keeper of the light, and you became a daughter of the dark. I do not know if you still remember the way to this lighthouse, or if you ever cared to find it.

I have watched the sea for years, and I have seen many storms. Some pass with little more than a whisper. Others break upon the rocks with a fury that shakes the very earth. But tonight, I feel the air grow thick with something unnatural. The wind does not merely howl—it *screams*. The waves do not merely crash—they *rage*. I fear this storm will not pass. I fear it will take us all.

I have not told you this before,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, five years of watching the light burn through the night, and five years of wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice.

I have not spoken to you since the day you stormed out, slamming the door behind you with such fury that the very air seemed to shudder. You called me a fool, a drunkard, a man who had forgotten how to love. And I let you go. I thought it was for your own good, that you would be better off without me. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was terribly, terribly wrong.

I have not been the same since. The lighthouse has become my only companion, and the sea has grown colder in my heart. I still light the beacon each night, though I wonder if it matters anymore. The world moves on, and I am left behind, a relic of a time long gone.

But tonight—tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen. The sky is black as ink, and the waves rise like mountains. I have lit the lamp

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and I have not dared to reply. The sea has been my only companion, and the wind has been my confidant. Yet tonight, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself reaching for words, not for the sake of reconciliation, but for the sake of peace.

You were young when you left, Eleanor. Too young to understand that a man’s duty is not always a choice, but a calling. I was not cruel in leaving you behind, nor was I cruel in keeping the light burning when the world turned its back on me. The lighthouse is not merely a building—it is a promise. A promise to those who sail the dark waters, to those who cannot see the shore, to those who must trust that someone is watching.

You remember the stories I told you as a child, of the sea’s wrath and the light’s mercy. You were always so eager to leave the shore, to chase the horizon, to find your own path. But I feared you would not understand that some paths are not meant to be walked alone. That some places are not meant to be left behind.

I have not spoken of you in five years, not because I have forgotten you, but because I feared that to speak would be to invite the past to

**April 12th, 1894**  
**The Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old stone tower. The sea roars with a fury I have not seen in many a year, and the waves, as if possessed by some cruel god, lash against the cliffs with a vengeance. I have stood watch for over twenty years, and never have I felt the weight of the world upon my shoulders as I do now.  

You were never meant to be the daughter of a lighthouse keeper. You were meant for more—more than the salt air and the ever-watchful eye. You were meant for the grandeur of London society, for the ballrooms and the fine gowns, for the life that I could not offer you. I was a man of duty, of duty alone, and I allowed that duty to blind me to the heart of the matter.  

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a single word from you. You called me a fool, a man who had forsaken you for the sea. And perhaps you were right. I was a fool to think that the light of the lighthouse would ever be enough to guide you back. I was a fool to think that the sea would ever be enough to keep me from you.  

But tonight—tonight

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, the sea roiling as if it too knows the sorrow that has long been in my heart. The lantern’s light flickers, though I’ve kept it burning all night, as I have always done—steadfast, unyielding, a beacon in the dark. But tonight, it feels more like a reminder of what I have lost than a promise of guidance.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that hollow shell of a home where I once found solace in the rhythm of the tides. I have not spoken to you since the day you walked out, your face like stone, your voice like ice. I have not written, nor have I dared to hope. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with fury, I find myself writing as if the words might carry through the storm to you—though I fear they will never reach you.

I have always been a man of quiet strength, of duty, of silence. You once called me a "grim old man," and I suppose you were not far from the truth. But I have never been cruel, Eleanor. I have never wanted to hurt you. I only wanted to protect you, to keep you safe from the world that would twist your heart and break your spirit. And yet, I failed you. I failed you in the only way

My Dearest Eliza,

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with a fury I have not heard in many years. The lantern is lit, as it must be, though the light may not reach the shore this night. I have no illusions of survival, not when the sea is so angry. But if I do not make it, I would have you know that I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to think of you.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of your name on my lips like a prayer. I have tried to write, to reach you, but the sea has taken my words, and the world has been too busy to listen. You were a child when you left, and I, a man who had long since given up on love, on family, on anything that might bring me peace. But you were my light, my one true beacon in the dark.

I have always known that you would not stay. You were not the kind of girl to be bound to a place, to a man, to a life. You were too bright, too restless, too full of dreams that would not fit in the confines of a lighthouse. I did not want to hold you back, but I did not know how to let you go. And when you did, I told myself that it was for

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the glass of this old lighthouse. The sea roars, and the sky is a canvas of black and fury, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I have not seen you in five years, and yet, in this moment, I feel your absence more keenly than ever.

I do not know if I shall survive this night, child. The storm is unlike any I have ever known. The waves claw at the cliffs, the wind screams through the cracks in the stone, and the light—my light—has flickered more than once. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I know when it is not merely angry, but desperate. It is as if the very soul of the ocean is in turmoil, and I fear that I, too, may be swallowed by it.

You were always so quick to leave, Eleanor. I remember the day you told me you would not return. You said I had no place in your life, that I was a relic of a time you no longer wished to remember. I did not argue. I did not beg. I simply nodded, and let the door close behind you. I did not know then that you would never come back.

But now, as I sit here, the light dimming, I wonder if you ever truly left. Perhaps you were never

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now rages upon the cliffs. Five years have passed since I last set pen to paper, and yet the silence between us has grown colder than the sea in winter. I have not spoken to you, nor have you spoken to me, and I fear the silence may soon be all that remains of us.

I have been the keeper of this lighthouse for many years now, and though the sea has always been my companion, it is not the sea that troubles me tonight. It is the thought of you—of your face, your voice, the way you once looked up at me with those wide, searching eyes, as if hoping I might understand the storm that rages within you as well.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. The lighthouse is my life, and I have given it my all. I have watched the skies for years, guided the ships through the darkest nights, and kept the beacon burning through every storm. But I have not kept the light burning within me—within us.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not forgiven myself for it. I thought that by staying silent, I might somehow preserve what we once had. But silence, my dear, is not the same as peace. It is only the echo of what was, and it grows louder with every passing day.

Tonight,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil’s own hymn outside these ancient stones. The sea roars with a fury I’ve never known, and I fear the storm may take more than the lighthouse tonight. I’ve spent five years in silence, but the weight of it has grown too heavy to bear alone.

You were ten when you left, and I did not stop you. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, too cold for a girl with your spirit. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was wrong to let you go, and I was wrong to let the silence grow between us like a rot in the wood.

I’ve watched the sea for thirty years, and I’ve seen many storms. Some come with warning, others with the cruellest of suddenness. This one feels different. The air is thick with an unnatural stillness, as if the very sky holds its breath. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but I fear the light may not be enough. I fear I may not be enough.

I’ve been thinking of you often, more than I care to admit. I’ve remembered your laughter, the way you used to dance in the rain, the way you would sneak into the lighthouse at night to watch the stars. You were a fire, Eliza—wild, bright, and impossible to contain. I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have stood at this post for many years, but never have I felt so alone as I do tonight. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn.

It has been five long years since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and five years since you left me with nothing but silence and the cold, unyielding sea. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to forgive you. I have tried to believe that you would return, that you would come back to see the light that has guided so many ships through the darkness. But you did not. And I have not spoken to you since the day you walked out of our home, your eyes filled with something I could not name, and your heart turned from the only father you ever knew.

I have watched the skies for years, Eliza. I have seen the storms come and go, the moon rise and set, and the stars blink out like distant memories. But I have never seen a storm like this. The waves crash against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundation of this tower. I fear I may not make it through the night.

I have not written to you, not even once, because I did not want to burden you with my

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in a place where words hold little weight. I have written many letters to you over the years, but none have ever felt as heavy as this one. The sea has been restless of late, and I find myself thinking of you more than I care to admit.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have not softened the ache of our parting, they have, at least, given me time to think. I have tried, in my own way, to forgive you. I have tried to understand. And I have tried to believe that you were not the cruel-hearted girl I once thought you to be.

The storm that approaches tonight is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is black as ink, and the wind howls as though the very heavens are weeping. I have lit the beacon, as I always do, though I do not know if it will be enough. I have always said that the light is a promise—to the sea, to the sailors, to the world. But tonight, I wonder if it is also a promise to you.

I have not forgotten the day you left. I have not forgotten the way your eyes hardened when you told me you would not return. You said I had no right to ask you to stay, that I had been a failure as a father. I do not deny that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, bitter winters since I last saw you, and now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast across the cliffs, I find myself wondering if I will live to see another dawn.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a man hardened by the sea and the weight of my own pride. I told myself that I was protecting you, that the world was too cruel, too full of temptation for a girl of your tender years. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was selfish. I let my pride blind me to the truth—that you needed a mother, not a father who could only offer the cold comfort of duty.

I have watched the sea for years, and I have seen many storms. But tonight, I feel something different. The sky is dark as ink, the wind screams like a banshee, and the waves rise higher than I have ever seen. I fear this may be the last night I will ever see. And if it is, I would not go without speaking these words to you.

I have always been a man of few words, but tonight, I must speak. I have no regrets, only sorrow—for the time we lost, for the love we let slip through our fingers, and for the daughter I failed to keep close.

If I do not survive, know that I did not abandon you.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1889**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and yet, the echoes of your laughter still linger in the wind that howls through the cliffs. I have not spoken to you in that time, and I have not written. But tonight, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the past, to the life we once shared.

The sea has been restless these past few days, and the sky has turned a sickly shade of grey, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. The waves have grown more furious, crashing against the rocks with a fury that sends shivers down my spine. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, and I have watched the light dance across the waves, a beacon for those lost at sea. But tonight, I fear the light may not be enough. I fear the sea may take me before I can say what I must.

You were a child of fire and light, Eleanor. Always so full of life, so full of questions, so full of a spirit that could not be tamed. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls outside like a beast in torment, and the wind screams as though it would tear the very stones from these cliffs. I have spent my life guarding this lighthouse, ensuring the souls of those who sail upon the waves are not lost to the dark, and yet tonight, I fear I may not be able to keep the darkness at bay.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last spoke your name without the weight of regret pressing down upon me. I have told myself that distance was the only way to mend what was broken, but I have come to see that it is not distance that heals, but the courage to face what we have lost.

I know you would not believe me if I told you how often I have looked out at the sea, imagining your face reflected in the waves. I have watched the light burn through the night, and in its glow, I have seen your eyes. I have stood in the cold, listening to the waves, and in their rhythm, I have heard your voice. I have not been able to let go, Eleanor. I have not been able to let you go.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You stood at the door, your small hands clutching a bundle of clothes, your eyes full of tears and determination

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from well, for I write it with a heart heavy with regret and a hand trembling from the cold. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with fury as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which this lighthouse stands. I have stood upon these cliffs for many years, and I have seen storms of all kinds, but none like this. The waves crash against the cliffs with a force that shakes the very foundation of the tower, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you in five years, Eleanor. Five years since the last time we spoke, and five years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have tried, I have tried to forget, to pretend that I had not spent every waking hour of every day since your mother’s passing watching the sea, listening to the wind, and keeping this light burning for those who might otherwise be lost. But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the way your eyes grew distant when you spoke of your dreams, or the way your voice softened when you called me “Father,” only to harden again when you turned away.

I write this not from a place of hope, but from the cold, unyielding certainty of a man who knows his days are numbered.

But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not told you what I have come to believe is true.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade. But tonight, I do not feel so certain it will be enough.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of dreams that haunt me more than the storms that rage against this rock. I have tried to write to you, but my hand would not steady, my heart would not speak. You were my light, Eleanor, and I have let it fade.

I have not spoken of you to the others, not even to the sexton who comes to check the lamp. They call me "Old Grimsby," a grizzled man with a heart of stone. But I am not stone. I am a man who once held a daughter in his arms, who once sang lullabies to her as the waves lapped at the shore. That man is gone, and in his place, I have become something else—something hollow.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and five years since I last saw your face, your voice, or the warmth of your presence. The world outside this stone tower has changed, but not as much as the silence between us has.

Tonight, the sky is dark and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The sea, once a calm companion, now roars with fury, and the storm that is gathering over the horizon seems to mirror the storm that has raged within me for so long. I have spent many nights watching the light flicker and the waves crash, wondering if I will live to see another dawn. And now, as the wind begins to howl with a voice that sounds like your name, I find myself writing this letter, not knowing if I will ever send it—or if you will ever read it.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even in your absence. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes full of sorrow and resolve, and your words—*“I have to go, Father. I can’t stay here anymore.”* I did not argue, nor did I try to stop you. I thought, perhaps, that you would return. But you did not. And I did not.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea, that old and restless beast, roars with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last spoke your name without it tasting of salt and regret. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, for the sailors who might need it, but I have not kept the heart of a father, and I fear it is now extinguished.

I do not know if you still remember the nights we spent by the hearth, when you were small and I would read you tales of the sea and the stars. You used to ask why the lighthouse stood alone, and I would tell you it was there to guide the lost back to the shore. But I never told you, Eliza, that I was the one who had long since lost my way.

You were always the light in my life, my little star, and I let you go because I thought it was for your own good. I thought I was protecting you from the darkness that I had become. But in truth, I feared the darkness within myself

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I have been a sentinel of the sea, not a guardian of the heart.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, rattling the windows and shaking the very bones of this old lighthouse. I do not know if I should have kept them, but I could not bring myself to let them go.

I have not been the same since you left. The light has grown dimmer, and I have grown older, though not by much.

My Dearest Eleanor,

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I ceased to be the father you once knew. I have not spoken to you, not written, not even thought of you. And yet, the silence has not brought me peace. It has only left me with the sound of the sea and the echo of your footsteps in the hallway, now long gone.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. The lighthouse, the light, the watchful eye upon the horizon. But I have also been a man who loved you, who watched over you with a father's pride and a mother's sorrow. And I have failed you. I do not know if I have ever truly known how to be a father. I was too busy with the light, with the sea, with the burden of responsibility. I let the world shape me, and in doing so, I let you slip away.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink still wet and the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself speaking to you as if you were still by my side, still listening, still *here*.
